mappings.rationaleAI: Ashoka the Great's Maurya Empire (c. 268-232 BCE), his conquest of Kalinga, conversion to Buddhism, rock and pillar edicts, and administrative structure directly correspond to B.5 (Mauryan empire) and B.5.a-b on Asoka's consolidation and Mauryan developments.

Alexander the Great's advance into Central Asia and India (327 BC) and establishment of Greek settlements directly correspond to B.4.b (invasion by Alexander the Great and establishment of Greek settlements).

The Ramayana as a great Sanskrit epic, with its themes of dharma and ideal kingship, and its cultural influence across South and Southeast Asia, corresponds to B.3.a (early Ganges cultures and religious development) and B.7.b (cultural and religious development in South India).

Al-Biruni's comparative study of Indian civilization in his Kitab al-Hind, written while at the Ghaznavid court, provides a crucial documentary source for D.1 (tripartite struggle in North India and Turkish control in Ghazna from 998).

Angkor Wat's design influenced by Hindu cosmology and Indian cultural traditions demonstrates India's civilizational influence on Southeast Asian civilizations, corresponding to outline A on the character and influence of Indian civilizations on Southeast Asia.

The spread of ironworking into India and its role in state formation and agricultural development provides context for B.1 (late Stone Age) and B.3.b (later Ganges cultures and caste system development).
